title: Cyclic Quad PASS: 5 witness pairs covering depth-2 and depth-3 tree nodes
_fixture_purpose: General theorem witnesses. For any two QA directions (d₁,e₁) and (d₂,e₂), the Ptolemy product formulas F₃=F₁F₂-C₁C₂, C₃=F₁C₂+F₂C₁ and conjugate F₄=F₁F₂+C₁C₂, C₄=|F₁C₂-F₂C₁| both produce triples on the G₁G₂ circle. The Brahmagupta-Fibonacci identity G₁G₂=D²+E² is verified for each pair. These are the integer algebraic cores of: (1) Ptolemy's ~150 CE chord-table sin/cos addition formulas, (2) Brahmagupta 628 CE identity for products of sums-of-squares, (3) QA Gaussian integer multiplication of directions. Verified exhaustively: all pairs with d₁,d₂≤10 (0 failures). Edge case: when gcd(D,E)>1, product triple is composite (e.g. (2,1)×(4,3) gives 25·(3,4,5)); identities still hold.

general_theorem.ptolemy_connection: PP and PC are Ptolemy's sin/cos angle-addition and subtraction formulas written as QA integer identities. The cyclic quadrilateral: 4 points on circle radius G1*G2 — two at G1-scaled positions, two at G2-scaled positions — satisfies Ptolemy AC·BD=AB·CD+AD·BC, which reduces to F3=F1F2-C1C2
general_theorem.brahmagupta_connection: BF identity is Brahmagupta 628 CE Theorem 8.36: product of two sums of two squares is a sum of two squares. This gives the QA direction multiplication rule.
general_theorem.uhg_connection: In UHG [cert 127]: QA null points α₁=(F₁:C₁:G₁) and α₂=(F₂:C₂:G₂); the product/conjugate triples correspond to the two intersection points of lines through the null conic — the Ptolemy quadrangle in null geometry.
general_theorem.algebraic_proof: G1*G2=(d1²+e1²)(d2²+e2²) = (d1d2-e1e2)²+(d1e2+d2e1)² — direct expansion. F3²+C3²: (F1F2-C1C2)²+(F1C2+F2C1)² = F1²F2²-2F1F2C1C2+C1²C2²+F1²C2²+2F1F2C1C2+F2²C1² = F1²(F2²+C2²)+C1²(F2²+C2²)... wait: = F1²G2²+C1²G2²... no. Let me redo: (F1F2-C1C2)²+(F1C2+F2C1)² = F1²F2²+C1²C2²+F1²C2²+F2²C1² = (F1²+C1²)(F2²+C2²) = G1²G2² ✓
general_theorem.verified_range: all pairs (d1,e1),(d2,e2) with 1≤e<d≤10, gcd=1, d-e odd — 0 failures

edge_case._note: When gcd(D,E)>1, product triple is composite. Example: (2,1)×(4,3): D=5,E=10,gcd=5 → triple (75,100,125)=25·(3,4,5). Identities still hold: 75²+100²=5625+10000=15625=125² ✓
